pages.title: Lesson 1: GTO Fundamentals
pages.excerpt: What "Game Theory Optimal" really means, and why it's the right baseline to study from.
headings: Alpha: the bluff's required fold frequency
Why we study GTO and not just exploits
pages.body: Lesson 1: GTO Fundamentals — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / 6-max / Lesson 01 6-max · Lesson 01 GTO Fundamentals & Equilibrium What "Game Theory Optimal" really means, and why it's the right baseline to study from. What GTO actually guarantees A Nash equilibrium strategy guarantees exactly one thing: against a rational opponent, you cannot lose. It does not guarantee that you maximize EV against every opponent — that's what exploitative play is for. GTO is the floor. Exploits raise the ceiling. Poker is a zero-sum game, so equilibrium strategies are symmetric in the sense that if both players play GTO, the expected value of the game equals its theoretical value (in heads-up, that's 0 for each player before rake; in multi-way spots it's your share of the positional advantage). Minimum Defense Frequency (MDF) When someone bets, you have to defend often enough that pure bluffs can't print. The formula is: MDF = pot / (pot + bet) Some common reference points: Bet size MDF Alpha (fold freq we give up) 1/3 pot 75% 25% 1/2 pot 67% 33% 2/3 pot 60% 40% Pot 50% 50% 2× pot (overbet) 33% 67% Important nuance: MDF tells you the defense frequency needed to prevent a 0% equity bluff from being profitable. Solvers defend less than MDF in practice because real bluffs have some equity, and because some combos are worth more than others as bluff-catchers. Alpha: the bluff's required fold frequency Alpha is the mirror image of MDF — how often a pure bluff needs to work to break even. Alpha = bet / (pot + bet) So a pot-sized river bluff needs to succeed 50% of the time. A half-pot bluff only needs 33%. This is the clearest mathematical reason why smaller bets are better bluffs: the bar is lower. Indifference and mixed strategies At equilibrium the bettor is indifferent about which specific bluff he runs and the caller is indifferent about which specific bluff-catcher he calls — every candidate in each class has the same EV. If one combo had higher EV, the solver would play it more, which would shift the opponent's best response, which would drop that combo's EV back to the group average. This is why GTO strategies are full of mixed frequencies (AsKh folds to a 3-bet 30% of the time, calls 50%, 4-bets 20%). The solver isn't hedging — it's maintaining the indifference point. The value-to-bluff ratio On the river, your betting range is polarized — value bets and bluffs. The ratio of value to bluffs, at equilibrium, depends on your bet size: Bet size Value : Bluff (optimal) Bluffs as % of bets 1/2 pot 2 : 1 33% 2/3 pot 1.66 : 1 37.5% Pot 1 : 1 50% 2× pot 1 : 2 67% Memorize these. They let you reverse-engineer a hand on the fly: "villain is overbetting the river, he'd need two parts bluff per one part value — does his line have that many credible bluffs?" Why we study GTO and not just exploits Stability. Exploits that work against one player lose money against another. GTO doesn't swing with the pool. Calibration. You can't

pages.title: Lesson 2: Preflop Opening Ranges
pages.excerpt:
Position changes your range by a factor of 3. Here's why, and how to
      memorize each seat's 100bb RFI.
pages.body: Lesson 2: Preflop Opening Ranges — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / 6-max / Lesson 02 6-max · Lesson 02 Preflop Opening Ranges by Position Position changes your range by a factor of 3. Here's why, and how to memorize each seat's 100bb RFI. The opening structure at 100bb Modern solver solutions for 6-max NL at 100bb with typical rake open to 2.5bb from every position. The range tightens as you get further from the button because: The number of players left to act is a geometric multiplier on how often you'll face a 3-bet. You are more likely to be out of position postflop. Rake is a fixed drag on small edges — marginal opens become losers earlier. UTG — 14% RFI Tight and linear. Mostly pairs, broadways, suited connectors 76s+, and the best suited aces. AA AKs AQs AJs ATs A9s A8s A7s A6s A5s A4s A3s A2s AKo KK KQs KJs KTs K9s K8s K7s K6s K5s K4s K3s K2s AQo KQo QQ QJs QTs Q9s Q8s Q7s Q6s Q5s Q4s Q3s Q2s AJo KJo QJo JJ JTs J9s J8s J7s J6s J5s J4s J3s J2s ATo KTo QTo JTo TT T9s T8s T7s T6s T5s T4s T3s T2s A9o K9o Q9o J9o T9o 99 98s 97s 96s 95s 94s 93s 92s A8o K8o Q8o J8o T8o 98o 88 87s 86s 85s 84s 83s 82s A7o K7o Q7o J7o T7o 97o 87o 77 76s 75s 74s 73s 72s A6o K6o Q6o J6o T6o 96o 86o 76o 66 65s 64s 63s 62s A5o K5o Q5o J5o T5o 95o 85o 75o 65o 55 54s 53s 52s A4o K4o Q4o J4o T4o 94o 84o 74o 64o 54o 44 43s 42s A3o K3o Q3o J3o T3o 93o 83o 73o 63o 53o 43o 33 32s A2o K2o Q2o J2o T2o 92o 82o 72o 62o 52o 42o 32o 22 Open raise Mixed (raise/fold) Fold Why each position plays differently Position RFI % Key additions from the previous seat UTG ~14% Baseline: 22+, A9s+, KTs+, QTs+, JTs, T9s, 98s, 87s, 76s, AJo+, KQo LJ ~17% +A5s, A4s, suited gappers (T8s, 97s), KJo, QJo mixed HJ ~21% +A2s-A5s all, K9s, Q9s, J9s, ATo, KJo pure, 65s, 54s CO ~27% +K2s-K9s filled in, Q8s, J8s, T8s, weaker broadway offsuit (QTo, JTo) BTN ~44% +Almost all suited, 97o-T9o, A2o+, K9o+, Q9o+, J8s, T7s SB ~40% (raise-first) Uses a limp+raise mixed strategy vs a solver BB; raise-only ≈ 40% Sizing: why 2.5bb, not 3bb 2.5bb is the smallest opening size that still denies the blinds a profitable call with their entire defense range. Larger sizings (3bb+) give up EV because: You risk more chips per chip of fold equity gained. You make 3-bets cheaper relative to pot, encouraging light defense. You inflate SPR with your weaker opens, which hurts your range postflop. Common leak: opening too tight from the BTN. If you're under 40% RFI on the button you are leaving money on the table. The blinds' rake and positional penalty make wide BTN opens one of the most profitable actions in poker. Small blind: raise or limp? Against a solver BB, the SB uses a mixed strategy: a ~25% limping range (small pairs, suited connectors, weak Ax) and a ~30% raising range (premiums and polar bluffs). Against a typical human BB that over-defends or folds too much, pure raising with a 40% linear range is usually more profitable. Start with pure-raise; add the limp mix once you

pages.title: Lesson 3: 3-bet and 4-bet Trees
pages.excerpt: The full preflop decision tree once an opener has been raised, from IP flat vs OOP 3-bet all the way to 5-bet shoves.
pages.body: Lesson 3: 3-bet and 4-bet Trees — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / 6-max / Lesson 03 6-max · Lesson 03 3-bet, 4-bet & 5-bet Trees The full preflop decision tree once an opener has been raised, from IP flat vs OOP 3-bet all the way to 5-bet shoves. Linear vs polarized 3-bets A linear 3-bet range is the top N% of hands by equity (AA, KK, QQ, AK, AQs, JJ, TT...). A polarized 3-bet range is a mix of the very best hands and hands that are too weak to flat but good as 4-bet bluffs (A5s, A4s, KJo, suited connectors that block the opener's calling range). The rule of thumb: In position, flat-calling is available → use a polarized 3-bet. The hands that play well as flats (AQo, 99, AJs, KQs) stay in your flat range where they realize equity best. Out of position, flatting is weaker → use a more linear 3-bet. From the blinds especially, flats don't realize equity well due to being OOP and closing the action vs a squeeze. 3-bet sizing Scenario Sizing Why IP 3-bet (CO vs HJ, BTN vs CO) ~3× Small sizing keeps bluffs cheap, positional edge justifies thin value OOP 3-bet (SB vs BTN) ~4× Charges a wide flat-calling range the maximum; OOP needs bigger pots to justify being OOP BB 3-bet vs BTN ~3.5-4× Closes the action, gets to realize equity at any price Squeeze (BTN 3-bets CO+caller) ~4.5-5× Each caller adds dead money + reduces fold equity, need bigger size 3-bet frequencies by position 3-bettor vs UTG vs LJ vs CO vs BTN LJ 4% — — — HJ 5% 6% — — CO 6% 7% 9% — BTN 7% 9% 11% — SB 9% 10% 12% 15% BB 11% 13% 15% 18% The 4-bet bluff frequency When facing a 3-bet, your 4-bet range is split between pure value (AA, KK, QQ, AKs) and 4-bet bluffs. The question every student asks: how many bluffs? At equilibrium, you 4-bet roughly 1 bluff per 1 value when the 3-bet is OOP and geometric sizing applies. Against an IP 3-bet, drop to 1 bluff per 1.5 value because opponent's range is more condensed on value and bluffs get less fold equity. Which bluffs? (Blocker selection) The best 4-bet bluffs have three qualities: Block villain's value combos. A5s blocks AA, AK. KQs blocks KK, QQ. Unblock his folding region. 72o is not a 4-bet bluff — it doesn't block anything. Some equity when called. A5s has ~30% vs AA. Ideal bluff backup. Standard 4-bet bluffs from most positions: BTN vs CO 3-bet: A5s, A4s, some A3s (blocker to his 4-bet fold region KQo/AJo) SB vs BTN 3-bet: A5s-A2s, some AJo and KQs mixed UTG vs anyone: Rarely 4-bet bluff — range is already polar. Mostly pure value (AA/KK/QQ/AKs). Flat-calling AA? (Sometimes yes) A solver will flat a small percentage of AA in cold-call spots (BTN flat of UTG open, for example) to protect the rest of its flatting range. If AA is always 3-bet, then when the BTN flats, the opener knows premium hands are gone from the flat range and can attack it. A ~10% flat of AA keeps the range balanced. Practical rule: against weak pools, don't bother. Always 3-bet AA for value. The tiny theoretical loss is dw

pages.title: Lesson 4: Continuation Betting Theory
pages.excerpt: Range advantage, nut advantage, SPR, and why "c-bet every flop" is a decade-old leak.
headings: When to check as the PFR
pages.body: Lesson 4: Continuation Betting Theory — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / 6-max / Lesson 04 6-max · Lesson 04 Continuation Betting Theory Range advantage, nut advantage, SPR, and why "c-bet every flop" is a decade-old leak. Two advantages, not one Whether you c-bet depends on two independent properties of the flop: Range advantage: whose range has more total equity on this flop? Nut advantage: whose range has more of the very best hands (sets, two pair, straights)? You can have range advantage without nut advantage, and vice versa. Treating them the same is the most common c-bet mistake. The four board-texture archetypes Flop example Who has range adv? Who has nut adv? PFR strategy K72r PFR (big) PFR (big) Range c-bet small (~33%) AK4r PFR (massive) PFR Range c-bet 25-33%, near 100% freq T98ss Villain (he has all the connectors) Villain Check ~70%, c-bet only strong made hands + draws 986ss (SB vs BTN) Slight for caller Roughly even Polar bet: nut hands + select bluffs, check the rest Range c-bet vs polar c-bet Two distinct sizings, two distinct purposes: Small c-bet (~25-33% pot) — "range bet" Used when you have a big range advantage. Bets close to your entire range, including air. Denies equity to villain's weak Ax and middling pairs. Minimum defense frequency required from villain: 75%. Big c-bet (~75-100% pot) — "polar bet" Used when you have nut advantage on a dynamic board. Bets a polarized range: sets + straights + select combo draws as bluffs. Checks everything medium: top pair good kicker, pocket pairs, gutshots. MDF drops to ~57%. Villain folds the middle, calls the top. Quick diagnostic: if you're c-betting AKx flops pot , you're burning value. Villain's range is too weak to call a big bet; you make more using a small size and betting with your whole range. SPR and commitment Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) determines how geometric your postflop sizing needs to be. At 100bb in a single-raised pot, SPR is roughly 11. In a 3-bet pot, SPR drops to ~4. In a 4-bet pot, SPR is ~1. SPR Typical pot Geometric river sizing ~11 (SRP) 6bb 33% flop → 66% turn → pot river ~4 (3BP) 24bb 50% flop → 75% turn → pot river ~1 (4BP) ~50bb 50% flop shove-equivalent The idea: if you want to get stacks in by the river with your value hands, divide the pot into thirds and bet the stack in proportional streets. This produces natural "geometric" sizings that commit you correctly. When to check as the PFR You check range (or nearly range) when villain's range is equity-favored on the flop. Classic BB-defender-favored flops vs UTG open: T98, 987, 876, 765 — BB has all the two-pair, straight, and set combos. UTG's AA/KK is actually behind here. 654, 543, 432 (rare in practice) — pure BB flops. 555, 444, 222 — paired boards where both ranges have equal sets; PFR's c-bet loses fold equity. J86, 974 rainbow middling — low equity edge, villain's suited connectors thrive. On these, check 70-100% of the time. Your bets come with str

pages.title: Lesson 5: Turn and River Play
pages.excerpt: Barreling, overbets, and how to construct a river range that villain's bluff-catchers can't beat.
headings: The turn: where ranges get refined
pages.body: Lesson 5: Turn and River Play — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / 6-max / Lesson 05 6-max · Lesson 05 Turn and River Play Barreling, overbets, and how to construct a river range that villain's bluff-catchers can't beat. The turn: where ranges get refined Roughly 50% of your flop range folds to the turn barrel. That means every turn decision prunes your range. A GTO turn plan has three components: Continuing range: which combos stay in your betting line? Sizing: small (blocker/denial) or big (polar)? Give-up candidates: which flop bluffs with no equity don't double-barrel? Turn barrel criteria (in order of importance) Turn card changes equity? Overcards and flush/straight completers tighten villain's range. Turn card helps you more than villain? Compare how many combos each side gains. Blocker quality? Turn cards that pair villain's range (K-high texture turns a K) drop barrel frequency, since villain now has more calls. Equity when called? A naked gutshot is a better bluff than a king-high with no draw. Overbetting An overbet (125-200% pot) appears in solver solutions whenever one side has a massive nut advantage and villain has too many medium-strength hands in his range. The conditions: You have sets, straights, flushes. Villain has only top pair. The pot is deep enough for the overbet to still threaten stacks. The board is static — no more key cards coming. Classic overbet spots: BTN vs BB, 9s6h2d-Js turn: BTN has all the JJ/JT/J9 combos. Overbet pot. UTG vs BB 3-bet pot, K72r-K brick turn: UTG has all AA/KK/AK. Overbet for thin value. Any paired river after SRP checked down flop + turn bet by PFR: PFR polarizes the river with overbet shoves. Overbet requires a bluff mix. If you overbet 125% pot, you need ~1 bluff per 1.1 value combos. That's a lot of bluffs. If you don't have credible bluff candidates, overbetting only for value is exploitable. River: the polarization endgame On the river, every hand is either value, bluff, or give-up. No more equity realization, no more draws. Your range collapses to three categories. The 2:1 rule (half-pot) and variants From Lesson 1, the value:bluff ratio at each sizing: River size Value : Bluff MDF for caller 1/3 pot 3 : 1 75% 1/2 pot 2 : 1 67% Pot 1 : 1 50% 2× pot (overbet) 1 : 2 33% Picking river bluffs: blockers dominate On the river, bluff selection is almost entirely about blockers, because every candidate has 0% equity when called. The best bluffs: Block villain's strongest calls. On a flush-completed river, bluff with the Ax of the flush suit — blocks nut flushes. Unblock his folds. If villain's folding range is missed draws, hold a card that unblocks those missed draws. Low showdown value. Don't turn a bluff-catcher (ace-high) into a bluff — that hand is more valuable as a check-call. Calling rivers: bluff-catcher selection You don't call with your best hand, and you don't call with your worst. You call with the correct amount of hands — the ones that

pages.title: Lesson 6: Blockers & Exploits
pages.excerpt: The final piece of GTO: choosing combos intelligently, and deviating from equilibrium when the pool demands it.
headings: Good blocker vs bad blocker — the four-quadrant chart
Unblockers: the other side of the coin
pages.body: Lesson 6: Blockers & Exploits — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / 6-max / Lesson 06 6-max · Lesson 06 Blockers & Exploitative Adjustments The final piece of GTO: choosing combos intelligently, and deviating from equilibrium when the pool demands it. What a blocker actually is A blocker is a card in your hand that reduces the number of specific combos villain can hold. Because poker uses a fixed 52-card deck, every card you see removes combos from everyone else. Examples: You hold A♠ on a 3-flush board. Villain now has 3 combos of the nut flush instead of 4. You hold K♠ on K♣Q♦J♥7♣ river. Villain has 3 combos of KQ instead of 6. You hold A♣ blocking AK on an A-high board. Villain has 8 AK combos instead of 12. Good blocker vs bad blocker — the four-quadrant chart Blocks value Unblocks bluffs/folds Best bluff ✓ ✓ Ok bluff ✓ ✗ Bad bluff ✗ ✓ Worst bluff ✗ ✗ Concrete example: river bluff on K♠T♠7♥2♣J♠ You open BTN, BB calls. You c-bet flop, check turn, face a turn lead. River completes the spade flush. BB is strong. You're considering a big bluff. Best bluff: A♠x (or Q♠x blocking QxJs, KsQs). Blocks the nut flush and AsKs. Bad bluff: 8♥6♦ — unblocks every flush, unblocks JT (two pair). Villain has his whole range. Folds turn in solver solutions. Unblockers: the other side of the coin You also want to unblock villain's folding range. If villain folds missed straight draws, a hand like Q♠9♠ on A-K-x-x-x unblocks those draws (T9, J9, QJ combos) — but it's a good bluff. If you hold KQ, you block half those missed draws, which makes the bluff worse. The counter-intuitive insight: strong hands are bad bluff candidates . A hand like KQ on AxJx7x2x9x has some showdown value, and by betting it as a bluff, you remove combos from villain's folding range. Your check-calls become stronger; your bluffs become weaker. Exploitative adjustments GTO is a starting point. Once you understand equilibrium, every leak your opponent has is a dollar sign waiting to be collected. Here are the adjustments that matter most by player type: Pool-wide leak: folds too much to 3-bets Typical online 200NL pools over-fold to 3-bets from the blinds. If villain is folding 70% to your SB 3-bet (equilibrium is ~60%), expand your bluff 3-bet range. Add: All suited Ax (A9s down to A2s) as bluffs instead of just A5s-A2s KJo, QJo as light 3-bets in position Stop flatting weak suited broadways (KTs, QTs) — 3-bet them instead Pool-wide leak: overfolds turn to double-barrel Many regs c-bet flop wide but then surrender turns too quickly. Against these players, when you bet flop and fire turn, bet smaller (50% pot instead of 75%) — the fold equity is already there, save money. Conversely, when they c-bet flop and barrel turn, call wider: their turn range is value-heavy but their river giveups are frequent. Player type: the station (calls too much) Shrink your bluff frequencies; expand thin value. Bet bigger with value — they'll still call. Don't run thr

pages.title: 6-max NL Cash Track
pages.excerpt:
Six lessons covering the complete GTO framework for six-handed no-limit hold'em
      cash games at 100bb. Ranges assume standard 2.5bb opens with 1bb ante-like
      rake structur
pages.body: 6-max NL Cash Track — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Home / Course / 6-max NL Cash 6-max NL Cash Track Six lessons covering the complete GTO framework for six-handed no-limit hold'em cash games at 100bb. Ranges assume standard 2.5bb opens with 1bb ante-like rake structure typical of modern online pools. 01 GTO Fundamentals & Equilibrium Why GTO guarantees a non-losing baseline and how MDF, alpha, and indifference actually work. 02 Preflop Opening Ranges by Position UTG 14%, LJ 17%, HJ 21%, CO 27%, BTN 44%. The "why" behind each number. 03 3-bet, 4-bet & 5-bet Trees IP vs OOP 3-bet sizing, the 4-bet bluff frequency, and when to flat call AA. 04 Continuation Betting Theory Range advantage, nut advantage, SPR, and the four board-texture archetypes. 05 Turn and River Play Triple-barrel construction, overbets, polarization and the 2:1 pot-size rule. 06 Blockers & Exploitative Adjustments Pick the right bluffs and deviate intelligently against real opponents. © Solver School

pages.title: HU Lesson 1: Preflop
pages.excerpt: In heads-up, the small blind is the button. Every hand starts with a big decision.
headings: SB as button: a very different environment
pages.body: HU Lesson 1: Preflop — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / Heads-Up / Lesson 01 Heads-Up · Lesson 01 HU Preflop: Open, Limp, 3-bet In heads-up, the small blind is the button. Every hand starts with a big decision. SB as button: a very different environment In 6-max, the SB is the second-worst position (BB is worst). In heads-up, the SB is the button — it acts last preflop (after BB's action) and gets position for the entire hand. This makes the SB the biggest winner in HU, full stop. Because of that positional edge, the SB plays a very wide range: ~80% of hands at 100bb. A tight SB is a losing SB in HU. The three-action tree at 100bb The solver's SB strategy mixes three actions: Min-raise (2bb): ~70% of hands. A small, wide open. Limp (1bb): ~10% of hands. A polar mix — strong and speculative. Fold : ~20% of hands. The worst offsuit garbage (92o, 83o, 72o, 62o, 52o, 42o, 32o, and similar). Why limping exists in solver HU Limping has two functions at equilibrium: Weak hands that can realize equity cheaply. K5o, Q7o, J8o — too weak to raise-and-fold profitably, too strong to give up. Strong hands to protect the limp range. A sprinkle of premiums (AA, AK) in the limp range prevents BB from attacking it with big raises. Should you limp in practice? Against a solver BB, yes — the EV gain is real. Against a human BB who over-raises limps or never checks back premium hands, pure min-raise with a 75% linear range is simpler and often more profitable. Start simple; add limping after 100k hands of volume. BB's response to SB min-raise BB faces a 2bb open with 1bb already posted and ~1bb equity from position reversal when OOP. The solver's BB strategy: Action Frequency Range Fold ~25% Weakest offsuit: 82o, 72o, 62o, 52o, 42o, 32o, 92o mostly Call ~60% The vast middle: pocket pairs, suited cards, most broadways, suited connectors 3-bet ~15% Polar: premiums (TT+, AK, AQs) + bluffs (A2s-A5s, 54s, some suited broadway mixes) BB 3-bet sizing: 3.5× to 4× Because the BB is OOP postflop, it uses a larger 3-bet size (3.5-4×) to (a) build a pot where its value hands dominate and (b) deny SB a cheap flat with hands like 65s or K7s that play well IP. SB's response to BB 3-bet At 100bb, facing a 4× 3-bet from BB, SB's options: 4-bet for value: AA, KK, some QQ, AKs. 4-bet as bluff: A5s-A2s (blocker bluffs). Call: Middle pairs (22-JJ), most broadways, suited connectors, suited aces that don't make the bluff cut. Fold: The bottom of the raising range — 83s, 74o, 42s, etc. The 4-bet sizing is ~2.3× the 3-bet (so ~9bb over a 3.5× to 4× 3-bet). This leaves a clean SPR for postflop play in 4-bet pots. SB limp-then-raise trees When SB limps and BB raises (isoing the limp), SB faces a 3-way decision: Fold the bottom of the limp range (J8o, K5o) to larger iso sizes. Call most of the middle. 3-bet limp-raise with strongest limp combos (AA, KK that were limped for balance) plus select bluffs. Common leak: BB raises every SB limp blindly.

pages.title: HU Lesson 2: Postflop Fundamentals
pages.excerpt: Range bets, defense frequencies, and why the HU SB c-bets flops at frequencies that look insane to a 6-max player.
headings: HU flops favor the raiser more than 6-max
SB as the PFR: high c-bet frequencies
pages.body: HU Lesson 2: Postflop Fundamentals — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / Heads-Up / Lesson 02 Heads-Up · Lesson 02 HU Postflop Fundamentals Range bets, defense frequencies, and why the HU SB c-bets flops at frequencies that look insane to a 6-max player. HU flops favor the raiser more than 6-max The SB open range (~75%) is much wider than a 6-max CO/BTN open, but the BB call range is also wider (~60%). Despite that symmetry, the SB's range is linear and capped differently : SB's range excludes the very bottom trash, while BB's range excludes premiums (they 3-bet instead). The result: SB has a range advantage on most high-card flops and roughly neutral equity on low/connected boards. SB as the PFR: high c-bet frequencies In HU single-raised pots at 100bb, SB's c-bet frequency at solver equilibrium is often 70-100% on high-card flops, using a small (25-33%) size. This looks aggressive but is correct because: SB has all the AA/KK/QQ combos that BB's calling range cannot have (BB 3-bet them). BB is capped at roughly AQ/KQ as "nut" hands on an A-K-x board. Small sizing denies equity to BB's underpairs (22-99 on A-high) cheaply. C-bet frequency by flop archetype (HU SRP, SB vs BB) Flop SB c-bet % Sizing A-K-4 rainbow ~95% 25-33% pot K-7-2 rainbow ~85% 33% pot Q-J-9 two-tone ~50% Mix of 33% + 75% 8-7-6 two-tone ~30% 75% polar when betting 5-5-2 rainbow ~70% 25% pot (range) 9-8-7 monotone ~20% Check, or polar 75% BB defense vs small c-bets When SB bets 33% pot, BB's MDF is 75% — a very wide defense number. Practically: Call any pair, any gutshot, any overcard with backdoors. Fold only completely airy hands with no backdoor equity (9-high no draw on A-K-4). Check-raise ~10% — polar: two pair+ plus select combo draws as bluffs. Check-raise sizing in HU BB's check-raises against small SB c-bets run ~3.5-4× the bet (to ~12-15bb into an 8bb pot). The polar construction: Value: sets, two pair, top pair top kicker sometimes. Bluffs: gutshots, open-enders, flush draws with backdoor additions, combo bluffs. Ratio: 1:1 value to bluff at flop — the bluffs have equity to realize. SRP as BB (when SB limps) In HU limped pots, neither range has a significant equity advantage — both are wide, SB's limp range is polar, BB checked its option range. Postflop play becomes about texture: BB often leads 25-33% on flops where its range connects harder (paired boards, low connected). Check-check is common — both players navigate to the turn before committing. SB's stabs after check are small and wide (30-40% when bet). 3-bet pots: smaller ranges, steeper decisions In a BB 3-bet pot, SPR is ~4. BB has the nut advantage (he 3-bet with the premiums SB doesn't have). But SB has position, which partially offsets. BB's 3-bet pot c-bet strategy A-high, K-high dry: Range bet small (33% pot). Bets 80%+ of range. Middling connected (T98, 876): Check ~60%. Polar when betting (75%). Paired: Bet small, ~80% frequency. SB's response Vs small c-bet:

pages.title: HU Lesson 3: Polarization & Overbets
pages.excerpt: Why HU rivers get so big, and the math behind the 2:1 overbet ratio.
headings: Overbet math — why 2:1 bluffs to value
When to check rather than overbet
pages.body: HU Lesson 3: Polarization & Overbets — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / Heads-Up / Lesson 03 Heads-Up · Lesson 03 Polarization, Overbets & Bluffcatching Why HU rivers get so big, and the math behind the 2:1 overbet ratio. Why HU polarizes more than 6-max In a 6-max 3-bet pot, both ranges are tight and the nut distribution is fairly symmetric. In HU, preflop ranges are so wide that by the river, both players have very long tails of weak hands — missed draws, ace-highs, weak pairs. This creates ideal conditions for polarization: the bettor's strong hands extract max value while their bluffs pressure villain's bloated bluff-catcher region. When does the solver overbet? Three conditions must all be true: You have a nut advantage. Not just equity — specifically the hands in the top 5% of the board's equity distribution. Villain is capped. His range can't contain the absolute top of hands (or contains them only rarely). Stacks allow threatening action. An overbet that commits The classic HU overbet spot: BTN vs BB, static turn SB opens, BB calls. Flop K♠7♦2♣, BB check-calls 33% c-bet. Turn Q♥. SB's range now contains every strong combo (KQ, KK, QQ, AK, AA) that BB doesn't have — BB would have 3-bet those. SB's solver action: overbet ~125% pot with a polar range of KQ+ as value and A5s/A4s blocker bluffs. Overbet math — why 2:1 bluffs to value Recall from 6-max Lesson 1: the value-to-bluff ratio depends on bet size. At 2× pot: Alpha = bet / (pot + bet) = 2 / (1 + 2) = 67% Villain must defend 33%. Your range: 33% value, 67% bluff → 1:2 ratio . That means for every value combo in your overbet range, you need two bluff combos . This is why overbetting is not a casual decision — you need enough credible bluffs to justify the big size, or villain over-folds and the bet is exploitable. Overbet shoves (all-in river) When stacks are shallow enough that an overbet shove makes sense (typically SPR Pot Shove Bluff % Value : Bluff 100 100 50% 1 : 1 100 150 60% 1 : 1.5 100 200 67% 1 : 2 100 300 75% 1 : 3 Bluffcatching HU overbets When villain overbets, you must defend MDF with your strongest bluff-catchers. Against a 2× overbet you defend 33%. Selection criteria: Blockers to villain's value. On a Q-high board where villain overbets, KQ (blocker to QQ) beats most of his bluffs and blocks one of the main value combos. Unblockers to villain's bluffs. You want missed draws to still be in his range, so avoid hands that hold his air cards. Minimum equity to call. Against a 2× overbet you need pot odds of 29%. Verify your hand has at least that against villain's implied range. When to check rather than overbet You do not overbet every node where you have nut advantage. You check when: Villain's range already folds too much — smaller sizing extracts more EV. You don't have enough credible bluffs to balance (usually because your range is condensed on value). Your range includes medium-strength hands that prefer smaller bets (poc

pages.title: HU Lesson 4: Exploits
pages.excerpt: Four leaks that define the HU population, and the concrete deviations that punish them.
headings: Why HU rewards exploits more than 6-max
pages.body: HU Lesson 4: Exploits — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / Heads-Up / Lesson 04 Heads-Up · Lesson 04 Exploitative HU Adjustments Four leaks that define the HU population, and the concrete deviations that punish them. Why HU rewards exploits more than 6-max Sample sizes are tiny in HU — you can be deep with the same opponent for 2000 hands, seeing every node they choose. That volume produces reliable reads quickly. It also means that if you're playing GTO and villain is deviating, you are leaving money on the table by not adjusting. Leak #1: BB under-defends preflop Solver BB defense to 2× SB open is 75%+. Typical players defend 55-65%. Adjustment as SB: Open every hand . Your bottom-range raise-folds suddenly become profitable. Use slightly larger sizing (2.5× instead of 2×) — villain's folds go up further, your value range profits more per hand. Reduce your limp frequency to near zero. Limps are a balance tool; they're unnecessary against an exploitable BB. Leak #2: BB under-3-bets Solver BB 3-bets ~15%. Typical human BB 3-bets 6-10%, reserving it for premiums. Adjustment as SB: Against the linear 3-bet range, your equity vs. 3-bet is much lower. Fold more to 3-bets. Tighten your call-defense range. Drop your 4-bet bluffs almost entirely. The blocker math breaks when BB only has AA, KK, QQ, AK. Widen your open (to every hand) knowing BB can't punish you. Leak #3: overfolds turn to double-barrel Perhaps the most exploitable leak in poker. After calling a flop c-bet, many players surrender the turn to anything that looks like a continuation. Adjustment as SB: Increase turn c-bet frequency to 85%+ on blank turn cards. Fire bluffs with almost any equity (gutshots, backdoors, two overcards). Reduce your sizing slightly — smaller bets generate the same fold equity against an overfolder, risking less. Counter-adjustment: when villain finally starts floating turns, switch to bigger sizing with a tighter barrel range (value + nut blockers). The moment you see one snap-call, shrink bluff frequency. Leak #4: never check-raises A common mid-stakes HU leak: villain calls c-bets wide but virtually never check-raises. Adjustment as SB: C-bet flop with your entire range — not just 85%, literally every combo. Small sizing (25%). Barrel turns wide since villain's check-call range is capped. Overbet rivers when you can cleanly polarize — villain's turn range has no protection for its value. Node-locking workflow Serious HU regs node-lock opponent tendencies in a solver and re-solve. Typical workflow: Collect 300+ hands on villain. Identify one specific node where their frequency differs from GTO by 15%+. Lock that frequency in your sim. Let the solver re-solve with the lock; study your resulting ranges. Deploy for one session; measure EV delta vs. baseline. The key insight: exploiting a single leak cascades into adjustments at surrounding nodes. You can't just widen one c-bet frequency — you also widen the turn barrel r

pages.title: Heads-Up NL Cash Track
pages.excerpt:
Four lessons. Heads-up is where GTO matters most — you can't hide in ranges,
      there are no spectators, and every leak shows up after 50 hands.
pages.body: Heads-Up NL Cash Track — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Home / Course / Heads-Up NL Cash Heads-Up NL Cash Track Four lessons. Heads-up is where GTO matters most — you can't hide in ranges, there are no spectators, and every leak shows up after 50 hands. 01 HU Preflop: Open, Limp, 3-bet The SB decision tree at 100bb — raise, limp, or fold. 02 HU Postflop Fundamentals Range c-betting as SB, defending as BB, and why HU flops favor the preflop raiser more than 6-max. 03 Polarization, Overbets & Bluffcatching Overbet shoves, the 2:1 ratio, and the blocker-driven bluff selection that defines HU rivers. 04 Exploitative HU Adjustments Node-locking typical leaks: underdefends 3-bet, overfolds turn, never check-raises. © Solver School

pages.title: Course Catalog
pages.excerpt:
Fifteen lessons across three formats. Work through them in order, or jump
      to whichever track matches the game you play most.
pages.body: Course Catalog — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Home / Course Course Catalog Fifteen lessons across three formats. Work through them in order, or jump to whichever track matches the game you play most. Track 1 — 6-max NL Cash Six lessons. The core GTO framework expressed through the most popular online format. 01 GTO Fundamentals & Equilibrium MDF, alpha, indifference, and what a Nash equilibrium actually guarantees. 02 Preflop Opening Ranges by Position UTG through BTN at 100bb — ranges, sizing, and why LJ is not UTG. 03 3-bet, 4-bet & 5-bet Trees Polarized vs linear 3-bets, IP/OOP sizing, and the 4-bet bluff frequency. 04 Continuation Betting Theory Range advantage, nut advantage, SPR, and small-bet vs big-bet board textures. 05 Turn and River Play Barreling triple ranges, overbets, polarization and the river value-to-bluff ratio. 06 Blockers & Exploitative Adjustments How blockers drive bluff selection and when to deviate from GTO against a live pool. Track 2 — Heads-Up NL Cash Four lessons. HU is where game theory becomes unavoidable. 01 HU Preflop: Open, Limp, 3-bet The SB's decision tree and why limping is part of a solved strategy. 02 HU Postflop Fundamentals SRP as BTN, SRP as BB, and the range-bet spots that dominate equilibrium play. 03 Polarization, Overbets & Bluffcatching Why HU rivers get so big, and the 2:1 ratio rule for overbet shoves. 04 Exploitative HU Adjustments Node-locking typical opponent leaks: folds too much to 3-bets, overfolds turn, etc. Track 3 — Tournaments (MTT) Five lessons. Where ICM bends every range you've ever memorized. 01 ICM Fundamentals Chip EV vs $EV, the Malmuth-Harville model, and how ICM pressure is born. 02 Nash Push / Fold Sub-15bb Nash ranges, the holdresources chart, and when to open-shove. 03 Stack Size Strategy Deep (40bb+), mid (20-40bb), short (10-20bb), and nit-short (≤10bb) play. 04 Bubble & Final Table How pay jumps compress opening ranges and why covering stacks get to bully. 05 Applying ICM Pressure Risk premium, FGS (Future Game Simulation), and deal-making math. © Solver School Glossary

pages.title: MTT Lesson 1: ICM Fundamentals
pages.excerpt: In tournaments, chips are not dollars. This single fact rewrites every range you learned for cash.
headings: Chip EV vs $EV: a concrete flip
pages.body: MTT Lesson 1: ICM Fundamentals — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / Tournaments / Lesson 01 MTT · Lesson 01 ICM Fundamentals In tournaments, chips are not dollars. This single fact rewrites every range you learned for cash. The core asymmetry In a cash game, every chip is worth its face value. Win 100 chips, make $1. Lose 100 chips, lose $1. Symmetric. In a tournament, chips have diminishing value. The first chip you stack from 50bb to 51bb is worth more than the 100th chip you stack from 149bb to 150bb. You can't cash out chips mid-tournament; they're just a score that decides your finishing position, which then maps to prize money. The Independent Chip Model (ICM) ICM converts chip stacks to expected dollar value by calculating each player's probability of finishing in each paying position. The simplest version (Malmuth-Harville) assumes each player's probability of winning is proportional to their chip stack. So if you have 40% of all chips in play, you win the tournament 40% of the time in the model. Second place probabilities are then computed recursively: given that player X wins, what's each remaining player's chance of finishing second? Worked example: 3-way with prize pool ($500 / $300 / $200) Stacks: A = 60k, B = 30k, C = 10k. Total = 100k. Player Stack % P(1st) P(2nd) P(3rd) $EV A 60% 60% 30% 10% $410 B 30% 30% 48% 22% $338 C 10% 10% 22% 68% $252 Notice: A has 60% of chips but only 41% of the equity ($410 / $1000). That 20% gap is the ICM tax paid by the chip leader. Risk premium Because chip EV and $EV diverge, you can't just play "chip-EV-neutral" in a tournament. Risk premium is the extra equity you need to break even in $ terms on a risky decision. Risk premium = required equity ($EV breakeven) – required equity (chip-EV breakeven) Example: calling an all-in needs 50% equity in chip EV. Under ICM, the same decision might need 57% equity because losing the flip costs you $30 but winning only gains $20. That 7% gap is the risk premium. Where ICM pressure is highest Stage Risk premium (typical) Adjustment Early MTT (200+ left) 0-2% Play nearly chip-EV (cash-like) Money bubble (15% of field left) 5-15% Short stacks massively tighten; big stacks open up Pay jump bubbles (final 3 tables) 3-8% per jump Medium stacks tighten vs covering stacks Final table (9-6 left) 5-12% Big stacks exploit short + medium; short stacks open-shove wider 3-handed 10-25% Extreme pressure; massive deviation from chip-EV Heads-up 0% Prize pool is fixed remainder; chip-EV returns The four rules of ICM Survival is worth money. Big stacks can afford variance; short stacks cannot. Covering stacks apply pressure. If you can eliminate me, you have leverage — even if we're both deep. Medium stacks suffer. Between short stacks that ladder and covering stacks that apply pressure, medium stacks have the worst hand of any stack. ICM dissolves in heads-up. Prize jumps are spent; it's winner-take-remainder chip-EV. Chip EV vs $EV

pages.title: MTT Lesson 2: Nash Push/Fold
pages.excerpt: When stacks get short, the tree collapses to two actions: shove or fold. Here are the charts.
headings: Why push/fold becomes the solved subgame
Open-shove ranges by stack (chip-EV, ante game)
BB calling ranges vs SB shove
pages.body: MTT Lesson 2: Nash Push/Fold — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / Tournaments / Lesson 02 MTT · Lesson 02 Nash Push / Fold When stacks get short, the tree collapses to two actions: shove or fold. Here are the charts. Why push/fold becomes the solved subgame Under ~12bb, opening to 2.2bb and folding to a shove is a disaster — you lose ~20% of your stack without a showdown. The solver solution collapses to open-shove or fold: a simple, 2-player zero-sum push/fold game that John Nash's equilibrium concept solves exactly. "Nash ranges" are the closed-form solution. Open-shove ranges by stack (chip-EV, ante game) These are the SB open-shove ranges vs. BB, assuming 1bb ante: Stack SB shove range ~% 15bb 22+, A2+, K2s+, K8o+, Q5s+, Q9o+, J7s+, JTo, T7s+, 98s 38% 12bb 22+, A2+, K2s+, K6o+, Q4s+, Q8o+, J6s+, J9o+, T6s+, 96s+ 45% 10bb 22+, A2+, K2+, Q2s+, Q5o+, J3s+, J7o+, T5s+, T8o, 95s+, 86s+ 55% 8bb 22+, A2+, K2+, Q2+, J2s+, J5o+, T3s+, T7o+, 94s+, 85s+, 75s+ 66% 5bb Any two suited, any pair, any ace, any king, most broadway 85% BB calling ranges vs SB shove Stack BB call range ~% 15bb 22+, A2+, K7s+, K9o+, Q9s+, QJo, JTs 22% 12bb 22+, A2+, K5s+, K8o+, Q8s+, QTo+, J9s+, JTo, T9s 27% 10bb 22+, A2+, K2s+, K5o+, Q6s+, Q9o+, J8s+, J9o+, T8s+, 98s 35% 8bb 22+, A2+, K2+, Q2s+, Q6o+, J5s+, J8o+, T6s+, 97s+, 87s 44% 5bb Any pair, any Ax, any Kx, most Qx and Jx 65% How ICM bends Nash The charts above are pure chip-EV (no ICM). At the bubble or final table, ICM pressure compresses both ranges — more so on the calling side. Shover tightens ~20%. The downside of losing a shove is steeper; marginal shoves with 85o become folds. Caller tightens 30-50%. Losing a flip busts; even AQ becomes a fold vs. a covering shove at extreme bubbles. The result: under ICM pressure, the big stack actually gets to shove wider than Nash, because the short/medium stacks can't profitably call. This is the origin of "big stack bullying." Open-shove from other positions Reference shoving ranges at 10bb from all positions: Position 10bb shove range % UTG (9-handed) ~14% MP ~18% CO ~28% BTN ~42% SB ~55% Re-shove (ISO shove) ranges When someone opens to 2.2bb ahead of you and you have 10-18bb, re-shoving is often better than flatting. You get dead money (the raise and the blinds) with fold equity against the opener's wide range. A rough re-shove range facing a BTN 2.2bb open with 15bb from the SB: Pure value shoves: 88+, AJ+ Thin value shoves: 55-77, AT, KQ, some AQ-AJ suited Bluff shoves: small suited Ax (A2s-A5s), some suited broadways (KJs, QJs) as mixes Total: ~18-22% of hands Shortcut for live play: if you can't remember a chart, use this rule — at 10bb, open-shove any pair, any suited ace, any broadway, and any suited connector 76s+ from BTN or later. Tighten by one tier per position you move earlier. Don't memorize Nash in isolation. Nash assumes chip-EV and a single-table context. At real tables with ICM, multiple players behind you, and p

pages.title: MTT Lesson 3: Stack Size Strategy
pages.excerpt: Deep, mid, short, nit-short. Your effective stack dictates what game you're playing.
headings: Deep (40bb+) — similar to cash, with adjustments
Effective stack — it's the smaller of you and villain
pages.body: MTT Lesson 3: Stack Size Strategy — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / Tournaments / Lesson 03 MTT · Lesson 03 Stack Size Strategy Deep, mid, short, nit-short. Your effective stack dictates what game you're playing. The four stack zones Zone Stack (bb) Primary tree Key goal Deep 40+ Open-raise + postflop Accumulate chips, implied odds matter Mid 20-40 Open-raise or 3-bet shove Maintain stack, avoid marginal spots Short 10-20 Open-shove + re-shove + min-raise mix Get chips in good, preserve fold equity Nit-short ≤10 Pure push/fold Find any spot with equity + fold equity Deep (40bb+) — similar to cash, with adjustments Deep-stacked MTT play most closely resembles 6-max cash. Open 2.2-2.5bb, defend BB wide, c-bet small on high-card flops. The key differences: Less flatting of 3-bets OOP. You can't realize equity in a bloated OOP pot as well as in cash — the ICM tail starts to bite. Tighter 4-bet calls. QQ becomes a mixed strategy (call/fold) vs 4-bets at 50bb effective; at 100bb it'd be a pure call. Check more rivers as the PFR. Getting to showdown cheaply with medium strength is more valuable when losing a big pot means bustout. Mid (20-40bb) — the 3-bet-or-fold zone The signature strategy at 20-35bb is the 3-bet shove . When facing an open at this depth, flatting is weak (you create SPR you can't manage), and a small 3-bet followed by a fold to a 4-bet is disastrous. So you play a binary strategy: Shove 3-bet with value + blocker bluffs (AA-88, AK-AJ, AQs, KQs, plus A2s-A5s as bluffs). Flat a narrow range (small pairs, suited connectors) only when IP with implied odds. Fold the rest. 30bb open-raise sizing At 30bb, open to 2.0-2.2bb (smaller than deep). You're not building a big pot — you're stealing blinds or setting up a cheap flop. Postflop, play a tight c-bet strategy: range bet 33% on high-card dry flops, check more on wet boards. Short (10-20bb) — the most complex zone At 10-20bb, your tree expands to four actions: min-raise, open-shove, limp (rare), and fold. The solver's choice depends on position and stack: Position Stack Default action with playable hand UTG-CO 15-20bb Min-raise (2.0bb) UTG-CO 10-15bb Open-shove premiums, min-raise others BTN/SB 12-18bb Min-raise wide (polar) BTN/SB 8-12bb Open-shove ~50% Facing a min-raise at 12-18bb The ISO shove is your friend. Re-shove with 22+, suited aces, suited broadways, AQo, AJo+ depending on position. Flatting is rarely optimal because postflop with 8bb in the pot and 10bb behind, you're not going to navigate any tough decision well. Nit-short (≤10bb) — pure push/fold Below 10bb, you've crossed into pure Nash territory (Lesson 2). No more min-raising, no more postflop. Find your spot and shove. Two additional nuances at 5-10bb: Steal-shove. From BTN/SB with fold equity against unopened blinds, you can shove very wide (50%+ at 8bb). Over-shove. Facing a min-raise, shove on top. The raiser is giving you ~3bb dead money to win; fold equity is massive.

pages.title: MTT Lesson 4: Bubble & Final Table
pages.excerpt: Pay jumps distort chip equity in specific, predictable ways. Learn the patterns and exploit them.
pages.body: MTT Lesson 4: Bubble & Final Table — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / Tournaments / Lesson 04 MTT · Lesson 04 Bubble & Final Table Pay jumps distort chip equity in specific, predictable ways. Learn the patterns and exploit them. The money bubble The money bubble is the single biggest ICM inflection point. One player gets zero; the next gets min-cash. The equity gap between 2nd-last and last is effectively infinite (min-cash vs. $0). Effect: everyone except the chip leader plays tight to ladder. The chip leader gets to open every button, 3-bet from the SB, and 4-bet-bluff short stacks who can't call without tanking their equity. Bubble strategy by stack Chip leader: open 50%+ from BTN, 40%+ from SB. Iso-shove over min-opens from medium stacks. Big stack (not leader): play chip-EV-ish preflop, tighten calls vs. chip leader's aggression. Medium stack (most risk): open tight, fold medium pairs to covering 3-bets, never flip if you can avoid it. Short stack: counterintuitively, open-shove wider than Nash — you get folds from the mediums who are ICM-scared. Shortest stack (the bubble stack): fold everything except premiums; the next blind might bust someone else and you cash. The pay-jump ladder Every pay jump is its own mini-bubble. Final-table ladders typically look like: Finish % of prize pool % gain over next-lowest 1st 22% +47% 2nd 15% +36% 3rd 11% +38% 4th 8% +33% 5th 6% +50% 6th 4% +33% 7th 3% +50% 8th 2% +100% 9th 1% baseline Notice how the bottom jumps (9th → 8th) are smaller in absolute dollars but huge in percentage. A short stack has far more to protect than a big stack does. Covering stacks weaponize ICM If you cover another player, you can bust them. That threat creates leverage even in spots where chip-EV would say call: Chip leader opens SB. You're BB with JJ and 30bb. Chip leader covers. Chip-EV: 3-bet shove for value. JJ is a monster. ICM: flat. Shoving risks your whole stack for a flip vs. AK-AQ and worse than coinflip vs. QQ+. The $EV of just calling and playing a postflop pot is higher. Final table stack structure A typical 9-handed final table has one big stack (40-60bb), a couple mid stacks (20-30bb), and multiple short stacks (7-15bb). The big stack's job is to pressure the mids while shorts do their own push/fold thing. Playing big stack at the FT Open 45%+ from late position. Iso-shove over min-opens from mids when you cover. Don't tangle with other big stacks — they can hurt you. Limp SB vs. short BB to keep them in bad spots; they can't fold to your option raise cheaply. Playing mid stack at the FT This is the hardest stack to navigate. Tighten preflop calls — especially vs. the big stack. Look for spots to shove over shorts who are limping or min-opening. Avoid flipping against covering stacks at all costs. Playing short stack at the FT Your job is to double up or bust trying. Ladder income comes from others busting, not from you surviving. Open-shove wider than mid stacks — ag

pages.title: MTT Lesson 5: Applying ICM Pressure
pages.excerpt: The capstone — risk premium, Future Game Simulation, and the math of deal-making.
headings: Wrap-up: the MTT player's mental model
pages.body: MTT Lesson 5: Applying ICM Pressure — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Course / Tournaments / Lesson 05 MTT · Lesson 05 Applying ICM Pressure The capstone — risk premium, Future Game Simulation, and the math of deal-making. Risk premium as a number We introduced risk premium conceptually in Lesson 1. Now let's quantify it. Risk premium (RP) is the extra equity required for a $EV-breakeven call above the chip-EV breakeven: RP = required %$EV equity – required %chipEV equity Risk premium scales with two factors: Pay jump steepness. Bigger jumps = higher RP. Stack asymmetry. The more villain covers you, the higher RP on your calls. Reference RP values at a final table Scenario RP Chip leader calling 20bb shove from short ~2% Mid stack calling 15bb shove from similar stack ~5% Mid stack calling 20bb shove from covering big stack ~10% Short stack calling 10bb shove from covering mid stack ~7% Covered stack on 3-handed bubble calling a shove ~15-20% FGS — Future Game Simulation ICM assumes the tournament ends right now in the proportions indicated by current stacks. FGS is an extension that models N additional future hands with positional and skill edges baked in. It's more accurate but slower and more computationally expensive. Practically, FGS produces slightly different equilibria at points where skill edge matters — a skilled player is worth "more" than their chip stack suggests, because they'll outperform ICM's lottery assumption in future hands. The main implication: if you're a better-than-average player at the final table, you should be marginally more willing to survive than raw ICM suggests. Applying pressure: the "unexploitable bully" Big stacks at a bubble have an unexploitable advantage. Their shoving and 3-betting ranges widen more than the calling ranges of mediums can tighten. Even if the mediums know exactly what the big stack is doing, they still can't correctly call — the RP is too high. Concrete bubble shove ranges for a 60bb chip leader against covered mids: Open raise BTN: 60%+ (adds K5o, Q8o, J7o, T7o, 84s). Open shove BTN with 25bb effective behind: 35%+ when mediums are very ICM-bound. SB vs covered BB: nearly any two — 80%+. 3-bet vs medium opens: shove 15-20% IP. Counter-play: if you're the medium stack being bullied, the only way to stop the bleed is to pick one spot and go. Find a clean 3-bet shove range (22+, AJ+, KQs type) and deploy it hours in. The bully has to respect the re-shove; their own ICM math gets messy if their open range is 60% and your re-shove range is now credible. Deal-making: the ICM chop When a final table reaches deal-making territory, ICM provides the baseline chop: each player's $EV according to the Malmuth-Harville model at current stacks. Variants: Full ICM chop: each player gets exactly their ICM $EV. Standard and mathematically fair. Chip chop: each player gets prize money in proportion to chip stack. Favors the chip leader. ICM + save for 1st: chop the minim

pages.excerpt:
Five lessons on how ICM, stack sizes, and pay jumps reshape every
      decision you learned in cash. The solver still applies — but the
      equity equation now includes dollars,
pages.body: Tournament Track — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Home / Course / Tournaments MTT Tournament Track Five lessons on how ICM, stack sizes, and pay jumps reshape every decision you learned in cash. The solver still applies — but the equity equation now includes dollars, not just chips. 01 ICM Fundamentals Chip EV vs. $EV. Why chips you win are worth less than chips you lose. 02 Nash Push / Fold The full sub-15bb open-shove charts for any position. 03 Stack Size Strategy Deep, mid, short, and nit-short. Every stack has a different optimal plan. 04 Bubble & Final Table The math of pay jumps and how covering stacks weaponize ICM. 05 Applying ICM Pressure Risk premium, FGS, and the geometry of deal-making. © Solver School

pages.title: Glossary
pages.excerpt: Terms that appear throughout the course, in one searchable place.
pages.body: Glossary — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Home / Glossary Glossary Terms that appear throughout the course, in one searchable place. Game theory Alpha The fold frequency required from the defender to make a bluff break even. Formula: bet / (pot + bet) . A pot-sized bluff needs 50% folds (alpha = 50%). Blocker A card in your hand that reduces the number of specific combos villain can hold. Holding the A♠ on a three-spade board blocks the nut flush. Equilibrium (Nash) A strategy pair where neither player can unilaterally deviate to improve their EV. The defining concept of GTO. Equity Your share of the pot at showdown, averaged over all future runouts. A coin flip has 50% equity. EV (Expected Value) The long-run average result of a decision, weighted by probability. Exploitative play Deviating from GTO to target a specific opponent leak. Unbalanced, higher-EV against the targeted population, exploitable against someone who counter-adjusts. GTO (Game Theory Optimal) A strategy at Nash equilibrium — it cannot be exploited by any opponent, though it may not maximally exploit any particular opponent. Indifference The condition where a specific hand's EV is equal across two or more actions. Indifference enables mixed strategies at equilibrium. MDF (Minimum Defense Frequency) The minimum frequency you must continue (call or raise) against a bet to prevent a pure 0%-equity bluff from being profitable. Formula: pot / (pot + bet) . Mixed strategy A strategy where a specific hand takes different actions with specified probabilities (e.g., 4-bet 30%, call 50%, fold 20%). Node-locking Fixing a specific frequency or range in a solver to model an opponent's known deviation, then re-solving to find the optimal counter-strategy. Polar (polarized) range A range composed of very strong hands and bluffs, with no middle. Typical of large-bet lines on the river. Range advantage Having higher average equity than villain's range on a given flop. Nut advantage Having more combinations of the absolute strongest hands (sets, straights, flushes) on a flop, independent of overall equity. Cash concepts 3-bet The third raise preflop (posted blind → open → 3-bet). By extension, a re-raise of any preflop raise. 4-bet, 5-bet The fourth and fifth raises preflop. 5-bet is usually all-in at 100bb. C-bet (continuation bet) A flop bet by the preflop aggressor. Check-raise Checking with the intent to raise when villain bets. A polar tool, usually reserved for strong hands and select bluffs. Donk bet A bet by an OOP player who was not the preflop aggressor. Rarely optimal at equilibrium but appears in some solver solutions. Equity realization The percentage of your raw equity you actually capture at showdown. Hands that can be bluffed off the pot realize less equity; nuts realize 100%. Float Calling a c-bet with a weak hand, intending to win the pot with aggression on a later street. Iso(late) Raising over a limp to isolate the limper and play head

pages.title: Methodology
pages.excerpt:
How the content on this site is produced, what's verified, and how to
      check anything you're unsure about.
headings: How to verify a chart yourself
Why not just replace everything with verified data now?
pages.body: Methodology — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Home / Methodology Methodology & Data Provenance How the content on this site is produced, what's verified, and how to check anything you're unsure about. TL;DR Most charts and numeric frequencies currently on this site are educational illustrations , not solver-verified outputs. They capture the correct qualitative ideas (which positions open wider, which hands are 3-bet bluff candidates, how frequency scales with position), but specific percentages and individual cell classifications have not been cross-checked against a real GTO solver run. Data provenance tiers As verified data is added, individual charts and claims will carry one of three badges: Solver-verified Generated from a documented solver run (TexasSolver, PioSolver, etc.) with input parameters available in the site repo. Published Taken from a third-party published source (GTO Wizard, Upswing charts, a named book) with citation. Unverified Educational illustration only — directionally correct but not solver-checked. Any chart or frequency without an explicit badge should be treated as unverified by default. How to verify a chart yourself Free preflop: GTO Wizard exposes a free tier with solver-verified preflop ranges for common stack depths. Compare cell-by-cell against the chart you see here. Run a solver: TexasSolver is free and open source. Feed it the same spot (stacks, positions, bet sizes, ranges) and compare frequencies. Differences larger than a few percent are worth investigating. Cross-read known authorities: Modern Poker Theory (Acevedo) for frameworks; Play Optimal Poker (Saulsberry) for accessible single-spot reasoning. Contributing verified data This site's data layer lives in data/ranges/*.json . Each file follows the schema below. If you solve a spot yourself (or export a range from GTO Wizard), drop it in and the site will render it with the appropriate provenance badge. { "id": "6max-utg-rfi-100bb", "title": "6-max UTG RFI (100bb)", "source": { "tier": "verified", // "verified" | "published" | "unverified" "solver": "TexasSolver 0.2.0", // required if tier=verified "params": { "stacks_bb": 100, "rake_pct": 5, "rake_cap_bb": 3, "open_size_bb": 2.5 }, "citation": null, // required if tier=published "run_date": "2026-04-19", "run_file": "solver/runs/6max-utg-rfi-100bb.json" }, "grid": { // 169 entries keyed by canonical hand notation "AA": { "action": "raise", "freq": 1.0 }, "AKs": { "action": "raise", "freq": 1.0 }, "AJo": { "action": "raise", "freq": 0.75 }, "ATo": { "action": "mixed", "freq": 0.35 } // ... }, "notes": "Solved on a single node; ignores flat-call from BB at open size 2.5x." } Why not just replace everything with verified data now? Building a full solver pipeline that covers every chart on the site is a real project — solving runs take minutes to hours per spot, and the full tree (preflop + postflop across positions, stack depths, and bet sizes) is thousands of spots.

pages.title: Tools
pages.excerpt: Practice the theory you learned in the course with these solver-backed tools.
pages.body: Tools — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Home / Tools Interactive Tools Practice the theory you learned in the course with these solver-backed tools. Tool · Visual Range Explorer Browse preflop GTO ranges across positions, heads-up, and Nash push/fold charts. Color-coded 13×13 grids with frequency stats. Open explorer → Tool · Practice Hand Trainer 12 scored scenarios across cash, heads-up, and tournaments. Pick your action, get the GTO answer with full explanation. Start trainer → Tool · Math Calculators MDF and alpha, pot odds, optimal bluff frequencies, and a full ICM calculator for tournament equity calculations. Open calculators → Tool · Verified Solver Browser Raw TexasSolver outputs reproduced in CI. Every spot is driven from a YAML config, solved by console_solver , and committed with full provenance. Open browser → How to study The fastest way to internalize GTO frequencies: Read the relevant lesson — start with GTO Fundamentals . Open the Range Explorer alongside it. Cross-reference every range mentioned. Run 10 trainer scenarios . Aim for 80%+ correct before moving to the next track. Use calculators in real time while reviewing hand histories — quantify every decision. © Solver School Glossary · Course

pages.title: Range Explorer
pages.excerpt: Browse 11 preflop ranges across 6-max, heads-up, and Nash push/fold. Hover any cell for details.
pages.body: Range Explorer — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Home / Tools / Range Explorer Range Explorer Browse 11 preflop ranges across 6-max, heads-up, and Nash push/fold. Hover any cell for details. Preset Raise / Open Raise mix Call 3-bet Shove Fold How to read these ranges The grid uses standard convention: rows and columns are A K Q J T 9 8 7 6 5 4 3 2. Diagonal cells are pocket pairs (AA, KK, ..., 22). Above the diagonal (top-right) are suited hands like AKs, AQs. Below the diagonal (bottom-left) are offsuit hands like AKo, KQo. Solid cells are pure actions; split cells are mixed strategies. All ranges are simplified solver outputs at standard sizings (2.5× opens, 3-3.5× IP 3-bet, 4× OOP 3-bet, 100bb deep unless noted). For exact frequencies, run your own sims. © Solver School

pages.title: Calculators
pages.excerpt: Live updating GTO math — MDF, pot odds, optimal bluff frequency, and ICM equity.
pages.body: Calculators — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Home / Tools / Calculators Calculators Live updating GTO math — MDF, pot odds, optimal bluff frequency, and ICM equity. MDF & Alpha Defender frequency required to prevent a pure bluff from being profitable, and the bluff's required success rate. Pot Bet size Pot odds Required equity to break even on a call. Pot before call Call amount Optimal bluff frequency For a given bet size, how much of your betting range should be value vs bluff at equilibrium. Pot Bet size ICM Equity Compute each player's $EV using the Malmuth-Harville model. Up to 9 players. Stacks (chips, comma separated) Prizes ($, comma separated) Tip: try 25, 25, 25, 25 with 100, 0, 0, 0 to see the winner-take-all distribution. The ICM tax on the chip leader is most visible when prizes are flat. © Solver School

pages.title: Solver Browser
pages.body: Solver Browser — Solver School Solver School Course 6-max Heads-Up Tournaments Glossary Home / Tools / Solver Browser Verified Solver Outputs Raw TexasSolver runs reproduced in CI. Each spot is defined by a YAML in solver/spots/ ; the workflow builds TexasSolver, solves, and commits the JSON. Pick a spot below to view the root strategy. Loading spot list… Want a new spot solved? Add a YAML to solver/spots/ and trigger the Solve spots workflow from GitHub Actions. Output is deterministic for a given config + solver commit. © Solver School Methodology · Glossary
